dialogue.utterance: 911 units, priority two for possible seizure at 653 MacCorkle Ave.
Hi, I'm Dana, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
She had a seizure, at least three of them in a row-each about a minute, with breaks in between.
She's not been on her Keppra since August, and she has been to the neurologist to figure out next steps.
Ma'am, can you hear me? Squeeze my hand if you can.
Can you open your eyes for me?
What is your name?
Can you squeeze my hand?
She's upright, with a patent airway.
Pulse is 113, strong and regular.
Respirations are 20 per minute, normal in depth.
Blood pressure is 142 over 89.
Blood glucose is 140.
Oxygen saturation is 100 percent.
Her breathing is unlabored and normal.
Let me check the cardiac monitor-EKG is currently unremarkable.
She is alert and oriented to time, person, place, and situation.

Do you have a history of seizures or other neurological issues? 19. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: signs_and_symptoms; Bystander: She had 3 seizures, each lasting about a minute. No fall or head injury, but she was acting unusual before. It's been a long time since her last seizure.
What were you doing just before this happened, ma'am?

I don't really remember exactly, but I was tired and just stopping to rest. Everything was okay before the first one started. 22. History of Present Illness; S.A.M.P.L.E.: medications; Paramedic: What medications are you currently taking?
Do you have any allergies?
I'm going to hook you up to a cardiac monitor for a quick EKG.
We've placed a 4-lead cardiac monitor to assess your heart rhythm. 26. Pain Assessment; O.P.Q.R.S.T.: quality; EMT: Are you feeling any pain now, and if so, how would you describe it?
You mentioned a headache-can you describe it? 28. Pain Assessment; O.P.Q.R.S.T.: severity; EMT: On a scale of one to ten, with ten being the worst pain you've ever felt, how painful is that?
Let me take a look at your skin. It looks normal, warm and dry, no bruising or signs of injury.
Ma'am, your pupils are reacting to light. No neck stiffness or difficulty swallowing noted.
No signs of injury or tenderness to the back or limbs, and you didn't fall during the seizures.
EKG is showing sinus rhythm right now. We'll keep it on for monitoring.
We're going to follow the medical-seizure protocol 3-12. That means we'll keep monitoring and ensure no complications.
I'm going to attempt to start an IV in your arm to prepare for emergency fluids or medications if needed.
Breathing is now down to 11 per minute, steady and within normal limits.
Oxygen saturation is at 99 percent.
Blood pressure is 127 over 85.
Pulse is at 94 now, steady and within normal limits.
Glucose machine shows no reading-possibly a battery or machine issue. Should we try again with a different test strip?
EKG still shows a normal sinus rhythm.
Let's take vital signs again now. Pulse is 98. Breathing is back to 20 per minute.
Blood pressure is 145 over 74. Pupils remain reactive to light.
Blood glucose still shows no reading. Oxygen saturation is 99 percent again.
How is the headache now that we're a little later in our assessment?
Your headache is 10 out of 10 currently, and it is a sharp type of pain.
Are you experiencing any nausea or vomiting now?
You vomited once during transport, but feel okay now.
Are you feeling lightheaded or dizzy at all?
Just to confirm, the Keppra is the only one you stopped, right? When was the last time you took it?
Are you fine with transportation to [CITY]?
We're moving you up to the stretcher now. You'll be transported safely.
You are secured with a 5-point harness during transport.

Ma'am, we'll turn you over to the emergency staff at [CITY] now. You had a GCS of 15 throughout the call-alert and oriented 4/4. You were initially unconscious but responded to voice and touch. There was no fall or head injury. Report given verbally to the nurse on scene.

She had three consecutive generalized tonic-clonic seizures since stopping her medication. IV access was attempted but unsuccessful. She is alert, oriented 4/4, and had a single episode of vomiting after becoming nauseous during transport. She is lightheaded and dizzy. She has no known allergies and is on Keppra, Flexeril, and mirtazapine. Report given verbally to the nurse on scene.
During transport, we monitored for any changes in condition.
Your pain level has not changed-it remains a 10/10.
No radiation or provocation of the headache noted in the last hour.
Have you experienced any further dizziness or lightheadedness during transport?
She was moved to bed 25 at [COMPANY_NAME] and care was transferred to a staff RN.
History of epilepsy and vertigo documented. No change in mental status noted during transport.
